rationale: Em-dash (U+2014) over-use is a FREQUENCY tell, not a per-occurrence one. 2026 detection research (Pangram; Wikipedia 'Signs of AI writing') is explicit that a single em-dash proves nothing — humans use them as a deliberate stylistic device — and that the signal only appears when LLMs sprinkle them formulaically throughout a text. The scanner therefore treats em-dash as a tier-3, density-only hint: individual occurrences are recorded (useful for a rewrite) but never surfaced as findings and never deducted linearly from the score; only an above-threshold density per 100 words raises the structural-tells hint. This avoids flagging legitimate, intentional human em-dash style.

Tricolon (rule of three: 'A, B and C') is a soft stylistic tell of AI-generated prose; LLMs over-use three parallel items to signal completeness. BUT a rhetorical rule-of-three is not reliably distinguishable from an ordinary three-item enumeration ('Python, JavaScript and TypeScript'; 'I read the docs, checked the code and fixed the bug') using surface heuristics — any regex narrow enough to avoid those false positives also misses most genuine tricolons. It is therefore recorded as a tier-3, density-only weak hint and the scanner emits NO individual finding for it (surfaced=false); a future aggregate/density pass may report tricolon frequency without flagging single occurrences as errors.

Negative parallelism ('nicht nur … sondern auch …' / 'not just … but (also) …' / 'not only … but also …') is a rhetorical template heavily over-used in LLM output to signal breadth or contrast. Human writers use it occasionally; in AI text it appears as a reflex on nearly every second claim. Detecting it helps flag text that performs balance rather than achieving it.
